title: Rolling 2x12 Shelf with Metal Angle Frame
summary: A three-shelf rolling cart with 2x12 shelves bolted to aluminum angle iron legs instead of wood — a genuinely different material system from every other rolling shelf in this catalog.
description:
This cart's legs are aluminum angle iron, not wood — a genuinely different structural material from anything else in this catalog's rolling storage, and one that makes for a light, thin-profile frame that still holds up thick 2x12 shelves. **Cut the aluminum angle with a jigsaw fitted with a metal-cutting blade** — a standard wood blade will not cut aluminum cleanly and dulls almost immediately, so swapping blades before this step isn't optional.

Predrill through both the angle iron and the wood shelves where they'll meet, then bolt or screw the angle to the shelves — metal-to-wood fastening benefits from a slightly larger predrilled hole in the metal than the screw's shank, so the screw doesn't bind while threading into the wood.

**The shelf spacing and even the overall height are genuinely open choices** — the cut list scales to your desired shelf length and your desired height without casters, so this build is really a formula more than a fixed size. Attach caster wheels to the base once the frame is upright, and if the finished cart feels at all wobbly, **the plan's own fix is a flat aluminum bar screwed to the back** — a simple diagonal or vertical brace that stiffens the whole frame without adding a fourth wood shelf.

tools.note: Cutting the aluminum angle, fitted with a metal-cutting blade.

materials.name: Aluminum angle, 1 in x 1 in, 8 ft total
Aluminum flat bar, 1 in, for back bracing if needed
Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.note: Or cut to your desired shelf length.

cutList.material: Aluminum angle, 1 in x 1 in, 8 ft total
cutList.note: Or cut to your desired height without casters.

steps.title: Cut the angle and wood
steps.body: Fit the jigsaw with a metal-cutting blade before you touch the aluminum — a wood blade will not cut aluminum cleanly and dulls within the first inch, so this swap is not optional. Cut four angle iron legs 24" long (or to your own desired height measured without the casters, since this build is a formula more than a fixed size), then file or sand every cut end: a sheared aluminum edge is sharp enough to open a hand reaching into a loaded shelf. Cut three 2x12 shelves 31-1/2" long with the circular saw, squaring each cut. Watch the yield on both: the three shelves take 94-1/2" out of a 96" 2x12, and the four 24" legs use the full 96" of angle with nothing left for the blade's own width, so measure your stock before the first cut and work from the long end.
materials: Aluminum angle, 1 in x 1 in, 8 ft total

steps.title: Attach the angle to the shelves
steps.body: Decide your three shelf heights along the 24" legs first — the source doesn't set them, so choose from what you plan to store — and mark them on all four legs together, clamped side by side, so a shelf cannot come out sloped. Predrill both materials at each joint: the hole through the aluminum a touch larger than the screw's shank so the screw passes without binding, and a smaller pilot into the 2x12 so the threads actually pull the joint tight. Drive the self-tapping wood screws — 24 screws across the twelve shelf-to-leg junctions works out to two per joint, which is what stops each shelf pivoting on a single fastener. Keep every leg's angle turned the same way round the cart so all four shelf ends bear on the same face of the L, and check the shelves stay parallel as you work up the legs.
materials: Aluminum angle, 1 in x 1 in, 8 ft total

steps.title: Attach the casters
steps.body: Turn the assembled shelf over and mount the four casters at the bottom of the legs. Note the geometry before you drill: a 4" caster's mounting plate is wider than a 1" x 1" aluminum angle, so the plate will not sit on the leg alone — back it with the underside of the bottom shelf, or with a block of scrap, so the whole plate is supported and its screws bite into wood rather than hanging off the edge of a thin extrusion. Set the cart down on a flat floor and check all four sit in the same plane before you load it.

steps.title: Add bracing if needed
steps.body: Set the cart down, load a shelf, and push on a top corner. If it racks, the plan's own fix is a length of the 1" aluminum flat bar screwed to the back — run it diagonally if you can, because a diagonal is what actually triangulates a frame where a vertical bar mostly adds weight. Be aware that the 2 ft of flat bar allowed for won't reach corner to corner across a back that is 31-1/2" wide and 24" tall, so either brace the section that is actually moving or buy a longer piece. Predrill the flat bar the same way as the angle — oversize for the screw shank — and land the screws in the solid 2x12 shelf ends rather than into the thin angle.
materials: Aluminum flat bar, 1 in, for back bracing if needed

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand the 2x12 shelves and ease every edge and corner — construction 2x12 arrives rough and its arrises are sharp enough to catch skin and clothing. Apply primer and paint, or stain and poly, and finish the undersides as well as the tops: thick stock that only takes on moisture from one face is what cups, and a cupped shelf on rigid metal legs will rock everything you set on it. Leave the aluminum bare — it won't rust, and paint won't stay on it without an etching primer.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: Rolling shelf cart with wood shelves and aluminum angle legs
